does: This paper develops a systematic five-stage methodology for identifying and validating causal features in transformer models, moving beyond mere correlation to establish genuine causal relationships. The authors demonstrate the approach on GPT-2 small for the Indirect Object Identification task, using activation patching to recover the canonical IOI circuit and sparse autoencoders to extract per-name selective features. They find that while activation patching on layer 9 head 9 fully recovers the task, the fifteen most selective features discovered by the SAE are only partially causal—ablating them together leaves the model 98% accurate. The work reveals a critical gap between feature selectivity (whether a feature responds reliably to specific inputs) and causal force (whether the feature actually drives model behavior), demonstrating this via complementary analyses including variance explanation metrics and selectivity-causal force anticorrelation.
connects: This work builds on decades of mechanistic interpretability research seeking to understand neural network decisions by identifying and testing causal components. It extends prior circuit analysis work, particularly the influential IOI circuit studies, by proposing a more rigorous multi-stage validation pipeline. The paper responds to earlier limitations in feature attribution methods by combining activation patching (a gold-standard causal technique from recent mechanistic work) with sparse autoencoders (an emerging unsupervised method for discovering interpretable features). The robustness testing phase directly addresses growing concerns about distribution shift in interpretability research, while the comparison between selectivity and causal force echoes broader questions in neuroscience about how neural selectivity relates to functional causality. The work integrates insights from multiple interpretability traditions: circuit analysis, feature attribution, and SAE-based approaches.
enables: This methodology establishes a template for conducting rigorous causal interpretability studies that could scale beyond toy tasks to more complex behaviors. The finding that high selectivity does not guarantee causal importance opens new research directions in understanding the actual information flow through model computations. The deployment integration stage suggests pathways toward practical applications like targeted model editing or selective knowledge extraction. The framework exposes differences between circuit-level robustness (how circuits generalize) and feature-level robustness (how specific features' causal effects persist), which could guide future work on model reliability and out-of-distribution behavior. More broadly, this five-stage pipeline could become a standard for validating interpretability claims, potentially reducing false positives in mechanistic explanations and establishing shared evaluation criteria across the field.
